strategic_overview: The working target should be the sharp classification p in [0,1/3] together with the isolated endpoint values p=1/2 and p=1. Necessity is already forced by equal weights: with m=3 and w_i=1/3, every p in (1/3,1/2) gives Pr[Bin(3,p)≥2]=p^2(3-2p)<p; with m=2 and w_i=1/2, every p in (1/2,1) gives Pr[Bin(2,p)≥2]=p^2<p. The isolated positive endpoints are real: p=1/2 follows from complement-pairing exactly as in Paper 1, 'An improved bound for the Manickam-Miklós-Singhi conjecture', complement-pair bound, https://arxiv.org/abs/1011.2803; p=1 is trivial; p=1/3 has an elementary three-colour coupling proof, e.g. https://math.stackexchange.com/questions/2279211/weighted-sum-of-independent-indicator-random-variables.

directions.name: Prove the candidate classification by isolating the hard interval
directions.description: First make the solver team treat the answer as the conjectural target: [0,1/3] ∪ {1/2,1}. The counterexamples above should be written as a formal lemma, because they also explain why no monotonic interpolation from p=1/2 can work. Then the whole proof reduces to the sharp sufficiency statement: for every p≤1/3 and every nonnegative weight vector summing to 1, the p-biased measure of the halfspace {S: w(S)≥p} is at least p.
key_ideas: If some weight w_i≥p, the event {v_i=1} already gives probability p, so every minimal counterexample for p≤1/3 has all weights below p.
The equality example for p≤1/3 is the dictator vector w_1=1, so any proof must be sharp with no slack at heavy-weight instances.

The endpoint p=1/3 is not just a binomial fact: couple three iid Bernoulli(1/3) vectors by partitioning each coordinate's probability space into three intervals; their coordinatewise sum is always 1, so one of the three weighted sums is at least 1/3, and symmetry gives the bound.
relevant_techniques: Paper 1, 'An improved bound for the Manickam-Miklós-Singhi conjecture', complement-pair bound, https://arxiv.org/abs/1011.2803: gives the full p=1/2 endpoint by pairing each subset with its complement.

The p=1/3 three-colour coupling at https://math.stackexchange.com/questions/2279211/weighted-sum-of-independent-indicator-random-variables is the natural analogue of complement-pairing and should be treated as a base case.

Paper 2, 'Tight Lower Bound on the Probability of a Binomial Exceeding its Expectation', https://arxiv.org/abs/1306.1433, and Paper 4, 'An Elementary Analysis of the Probability That a Binomial Random Variable Exceeds Its Expectation', https://arxiv.org/abs/1712.00519, are useful for checking that equal-weight tests do not hide further exceptional p-values.
directions.potential_obstacles: The function p ↦ Pr_p[w(S)≥p] is not monotone in any useful global sense because the threshold moves and the winning family jumps at subset sums. The fact that p=1/2 is true while p=0.4 is false is the main warning.

directions.name: Minimal-counterexample and threshold-family analysis
directions.description: Assume p≤1/3 is a counterexample and choose one with minimal support, then with p chosen at a right-hand discontinuity of the threshold family. On each interval between subset-sum values, the family {S: w(S)≥p} is fixed and its p-biased measure is a polynomial; therefore any failure should be movable to a subset-sum boundary. Analyze the critical sets with w(S)=p and try to show that either a heavy coordinate gives the bound, or the critical structure forces at least four comparable pieces, where direct binomial-type lower bounds have slack.
key_ideas: Reduce to all weights strictly below p; otherwise the event that a heavy coordinate is selected proves the inequality.

Work with the bad down-set G={S: w(S)<p}. For p≤1/3, three bad sets cannot have weighted union 1, since each has weight <p. The plain no-three-cover condition is too weak by itself, but the fractional-weight certificate is extra structure that should be exploited.

At a discontinuity p=w(A), the loss in probability comes from subsets A exactly at threshold. The m=3 equal-weight obstruction at p just above 1/3 is precisely a large singleton-threshold jump; for p<1/3 one should prove that all such jumps are compensated by multi-success mass.
relevant_techniques: Paper 15, 'On Sums of Independent Random Variables with Unbounded Variance, and Estimating the Average Degree in a Graph', support-reduction and merge philosophy, https://www.wisdom.weizmann.ac.il/~feige/Others/newmarkov.pdf: use its extremal mindset, even though the variables here are already two-point.

Paper 3, 'On Some Conjectures of Samuels and Feige', product-to-largest-weight and endpoint-reduction ideas, https://arxiv.org/abs/1703.05152: suggests that a true extremal proof may collapse arbitrary weights to one dominant coordinate or a small equal-weight obstruction.

Paper 5, 'Small Deviations of Sums of Independent Random Variables', two-point moment identities, https://arxiv.org/abs/1804.01529: useful for expressing structural constraints of centered Bernoulli summands if the threshold-family analysis is converted into moment inequalities.
directions.potential_obstacles: The space of weighted threshold families is highly nonconvex. A perturbation argument must handle many simultaneous subset-sum equalities, and arbitrary down-set extremal theorems are too weak unless they preserve the specific fractional weight certificate.

directions.name: Complemented Feige-Samuels exact-mean route
directions.description: Complement the Bernoulli variables. Let y_i=1-v_i, q=1-p, and T=sum_i w_i y_i. The desired inequality becomes Pr[T≤q]≥1-q for q≥2/3. Each summand has support {0,w_i}, mean q w_i, and surplus p w_i in Feige's two-point language. Try to sharpen Feige's exact-mean lower-tail machinery in the special regime surplus/mean=p/q≤1/2.
key_ideas: The dictator weight w_1=1 gives equality Pr[T≤q]=p, so the extremal form matches Feige/Samuels' one-large-variable examples.
If weights are grouped into blocks with comparable means, Feige's one-zero product estimate may give the exact p lower bound rather than just a universal constant.
The special Bernoulli structure is much simpler than Feige's general nonnegative variables: all surplus-to-mean ratios are identical, p/q.
relevant_techniques: Paper 15, Proposition 8 one-zero product bound, https://www.wisdom.weizmann.ac.il/~feige/Others/newmarkov.pdf: gives lower-tail probability from the event that at least one two-point variable is zero, after controlling largest mean and total surplus.
Paper 15, Stage 2 modified merge for exact-mean lower tails: particularly relevant because our complemented target is exactly T≤E[T], not T≤E[T]+δ.

Paper 3, Theorem 1.1 and Lemmas 3.2-3.4, https://arxiv.org/abs/1703.05152: the logarithmic normalization and endpoint reduction give a template for proving that only one-heavy-coordinate or evenly spread configurations matter.
directions.potential_obstacles: Feige's proved constants, such as 1/13, are far too weak for an exact p bound, and his theorem is for positive slack. The solver must substantially exploit the special Bernoulli and q≥2/3 structure rather than quote the theorem directly.

directions.name: Fixed-size layers plus MMS/fractional-cover input
directions.description: Condition on R=|S|. Given R=r, the selected set is a uniform r-subset. For r≥mp, set x_i=w_i-p/r; then sum_i x_i=1-mp/r≥0, and w(S)≥p is exactly sum_{i in S} x_i≥0. Apply MMS-type lower bounds inside that layer. This alone is probably too weak for the sharp theorem, but it can handle diffuse or large-m regimes and may reduce the remaining cases to bounded support or heavy weights.
key_ideas: Layer r has deterministic average r/m. If r/m≥p, the centered weights have nonnegative total, so fixed-size nonnegative-sum theorems apply.
The Baranyai divisible case gives a clean conditional lower bound when r divides m; central-element and deletion arguments give eventual MMS bounds.
Combine layer estimates with exact binomial tail estimates to prove partial results, then use a separate heavy-coordinate argument for the non-diffuse cases.
relevant_techniques: Paper 1, Baranyai divisible case and central-element iteration, https://arxiv.org/abs/1011.2803: directly applies after conditioning on |S|=r and centering by p/r.
Paper 1, order-statistic inequality: may certify many successful r-subsets containing large weights when a full MMS theorem is unavailable.

Paper 7, 'Nonnegative k-sums, fractional covers, and probability of small deviations', https://people.math.ethz.ch/~sudakovb/nonnegative-k-sums.pdf: Stage 0 identified this as the direct bridge between nonnegative k-sums, fractional covers, and small-deviation probability; if this route is pursued, deep-read this PDF first.

Paper 14, Pitman's 'Probabilistic Bounds on the Coefficients of Polynomials with Only Real Zeros', https://www.stat.berkeley.edu/users/pitman/453.pdf: useful if a layer or coefficient sequence can be made Poisson-binomial or PF, enabling Hoeffding, Darroch, and normal-approximation tools.
directions.potential_obstacles: The standard MMS lower bound gives only a star fraction r/m of good r-subsets. Summed over layers this is generally below p, so a sharp proof needs extra information from bad layers or stronger structural counting.

directions.name: Moment-polynomial certificate for the centered Bernoulli sum
directions.description: Let Z=sum_i w_i(v_i-p), so E[Z]=0 and the target is Pr[Z≥0]≥p. For p≤1/3, each centered summand has positive third moment. Try to construct a p-dependent polynomial majorant for 1_{Z<0} whose expectation is at most 1-p for every choice of weights, with equality at the dictator distribution.
key_ideas: The moments are explicit power sums in the weights: variance is p(1-p) sum w_i^2 and the third central moment is p(1-p)(1-2p) sum w_i^3.
A successful dual polynomial should be tangent to the two-point dictator law, because that is the sharp equality case for p≤1/3.
If fourth moments are insufficient, move to a higher even-degree certificate but keep the same two-point moment structure.
relevant_techniques: Paper 5, Lemma 2.2 quartic majorant Q_{ell,r}, https://arxiv.org/abs/1804.01529: supplies explicit one-sided polynomial majorants and expectation formulas in terms of moments.
Paper 5, Theorem 2.1 and Section 3 refinements: show how the sign of the third moment improves one-sided probability bounds.
Paper 5, two-point negative/positive third-moment identities: exactly match centered Bernoulli summands and may allow sharper p-dependent tuning than the paper's universal constants.
directions.potential_obstacles: Paper 5 itself warns that first-four-moment methods have intrinsic constant barriers in related problems. The present inequality is sharp at a two-point law for every p≤1/3, so a low-degree certificate may fail unless it is very carefully tuned.

directions.name: Equal-weight and computational guardrails
directions.description: Use equal weights and small-dimensional chamber enumeration as a diagnostic, not as the main proof. Equal weights already give all necessary exclusions outside [0,1/3] ∪ {1/2,1}; for p≤1/3 they should be safely above p except at dictator-like limits. A solver should run exact symbolic checks for m≤6 or m≤7 weighted threshold chambers to identify the true extremal patterns before committing to a global proof.
key_ideas: For fixed p and a fixed threshold family, the p-biased measure is independent of the actual weights; feasibility of the family is a linear-inequality problem in the weights.
Enumerating feasible threshold families for small m can reveal whether non-dictator extremizers exist below 1/3.
Binomial asymptotics and Chvátal-Janson monotonicity are useful for ruling out misleading equal-weight conjectures.
relevant_techniques: Paper 2, piecewise monotonicity and critical binomial CDF reduction, https://arxiv.org/abs/1306.1433: reduces equal-weight continuum checks to grid parameters.
Paper 4, floor-mean grid reduction and Robbins-Stirling anti-concentration, https://arxiv.org/abs/1712.00519: gives elementary rigorous estimates for binomial tails around their mean.

Paper 6, 'On the Chvátal-Janson Conjecture', https://arxiv.org/abs/2104.11971, and Paper 13, https://www.diva-portal.org/smash/get/diva2%3A1558617/FULLTEXT01.pdf: explain the fine lattice behavior of binomial probabilities at integer means.
directions.potential_obstacles: Equal weights are only a source of counterexamples and sanity checks. The desired theorem is about all weighted halfspaces, and weighted sums do not generally have the real-rooted coefficient structure available for Poisson-binomial counts.

key_obstacles: The main barrier is sharpness. Universal small-deviation bounds from Feige-type or moment methods are much weaker than the required lower bound p, while the dictator weight vector shows that no proof can afford slack. The second barrier is the moving threshold: as p changes, the winning family jumps at subset sums, so ordinary monotonicity in p is misleading. The third barrier is that the best-developed exact tools in the literature are either for equal weights/binomial counts or for fixed-size subset layers; arbitrary weighted Bernoulli halfspaces sit between these settings and lose the real-rooted/PF and lattice advantages.
recommended_starting_point: Start by writing a formal one-page reduction to the candidate answer [0,1/3] ∪ {1/2,1}: include the m=2 and m=3 equal-weight counterexamples, the p=1/2 complement-pair proof from Paper 1, and the p=1/3 three-colour coupling. Then attack the only hard part, p<1/3, via the minimal-counterexample/threshold-family direction. The very first technical lemma to try is: any counterexample with p≤1/3 may be chosen with all weights
